title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe: Make Black Mole
seo.metaDescription: Master the rich flavors of Oaxacan Black Mole with our authentic recipe. Learn to make this traditional Mexican black mole from scratch for a truly special dinner.

traditional Oaxacan Black Mole from scratch
best Oaxacan Black Mole recipe at home

seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole served with chicken and rice, garnished with sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of Oaxaca with this incredible homemade Black Mole. Perfect for a special dinner!

description: Mole Negro is the king of Oaxacan moles, a complex and deeply flavorful sauce with a dark, velvety texture. It balances a symphony of ingredients including chiles, nuts, spices, and chocolate, creating an unforgettable culinary experience. This traditional recipe brings the authentic taste of Oaxaca right to your table, perfect for a special family meal.

ingredients.notes: or a slice of bolillo bread

instructions.title: Prepare and Toast the Chiles
instructions.text: Heat 1 tablespoon of lard or oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Briefly toast the dried chiles in batches for 30-60 seconds per side until fragrant but not burnt. Transfer them to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20 minutes. Drain and reserve the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Roast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices
instructions.text: In the same skillet, dry-toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds until lightly golden and fragrant. Remove and set aside. Then, dry-toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, cumin seeds, and anise seeds until aromatic, about 1 minute. Add dried thyme and oregano for the last 30 seconds. Set aside.

instructions.title: Fry Aromatics and Other Ingredients
instructions.text: Add another tablespoon of lard or oil to the skillet. Fry the chopped onion and garlic until softened, about 5 minutes. Add the tomatoes, tomatillos, plantain slices, and raisins. Cook until the vegetables are softened and slightly browned, about 8-10 minutes. In a separate small pan, lightly fry the stale tortilla or bolillo until crisp. Add all these fried ingredients to the reserved nuts, seeds, and spices.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Base
instructions.text: Combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts and spices, and fried aromatics into a blender. Add 1-2 cups of hot chicken broth (or reserved chile soaking liquid, strained). Blend until a very smooth paste forms, adding more broth as needed to help it blend. It may take several minutes and batches to achieve a fine consistency. Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl, pressing down on the solids to extract all liquid. Discard any remaining solids.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the remaining tablespoon of lard or oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the strained mole paste. Stir constantly to prevent sticking and burning. Cook for at least 20-30 minutes, or until the mole darkens, thickens, and the flavors deepen. This step is crucial for developing the mole's rich taste.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Gradually add the remaining hot chicken broth to the mole, stirring continuously until it reaches your desired consistency. Add the Mexican chocolate and stir until it is completely melted and incorporated. Season with sugar and salt to taste. Simmer for another 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld. Serve hot over cooked chicken, turkey, or enchiladas, garnished with sesame seeds.

tips: For truly authentic Mole Negro, seek out Chilhuacle Negro chiles. If unavailable, use a combination of mulato, ancho, and pasilla for a similar deep flavor profile.
The straining step is essential for a smooth, velvety mole. Do not skip it, even if it takes extra time. A high-quality blender helps reduce the amount of straining needed.
Mole Negro improves with age. Make it a day in advance and gently reheat it before serving to allow the complex flavors to fully develop and meld.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Mexico with Oaxacan Black Mole, a dish that transcends mere sustenance to become a profound cultural statement. Known as Mole Negro, this intricate sauce is often hailed as the "king of moles" and is a cornerstone of Oaxacan cuisine, celebrated for its astonishing depth of flavor and velvety texture. More than just a Mexican dinner recipe, it's a labor of love, a testament to culinary tradition, and an unforgettable experience for anyone who tastes it. If you're ready to explore one of the world's most complex and rewarding sauces, prepare to be captivated by the magic of Oaxacan Black Mole.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole?

Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, is arguably the most famous and complex of the seven moles native to the state of Oaxaca, Mexico. Its distinctive dark, almost black color, comes from a careful roasting process of numerous ingredients, particularly a specific blend of dried chiles. Unlike simpler sauces, Mole Negro is a symphony of flavors – smoky, sweet, spicy, savory, earthy, and even slightly bitter – all harmoniously balanced. It typically boasts a list of 20 to 30 or even more ingredients, including various dried chiles, nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, vegetables, and Mexican chocolate. This isn't a quick weeknight meal; it's a dish reserved for special occasions, holidays, and family gatherings, signifying celebration and tradition. Its preparation is a meticulous, multi-step process that can take days, reflecting the deep respect and love imbued in every spoonful. The result is a thick, rich, glossy sauce with an unparalleled complexity that coats the palate and leaves a lasting impression.

## Ingredients for Oaxacan Black Mole

The extensive list of ingredients is what gives Oaxacan Black Mole its legendary complexity. While recipes vary slightly, certain components are non-negotiable for achieving its authentic flavor and color:

*   **Dried Chiles:** The backbone of Mole Negro. Essential varieties include chilhuacle negro (if available, though rare outside Oaxaca), mulato, pasilla mexicano, and ancho chiles. These are toasted, rehydrated, and contribute significantly to the color, smokiness, and heat.
*   **Aromatics:** White onion and garlic form the foundational savory notes.
*   **Spices:** A diverse blend including cinnamon sticks (Mexican canela), cloves, black peppercorns, cumin seeds, coriander seeds, and sometimes star anise or allspice. These are usually toasted to release their full aroma.
*   **Thickeners & Body:** Stale corn tortillas, bolillo bread (or similar crusty bread), almonds, peanuts, pecans, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds (pepitas) are toasted and ground to give the mole its characteristic thickness and richness.
*   **Fruits & Sweeteners:** Plantains, raisins, prunes, and dark Mexican chocolate (often containing cinnamon and sugar) contribute sweetness and a crucial depth of flavor, balancing the chile's heat and bitterness. Some recipes also include a touch of piloncillo (unrefined cane sugar).
*   **Acids:** A splash of apple cider vinegar or a ripe tomato can add a subtle tang.
*   **Fats:** Lard or a neutral cooking oil is used for frying the various components and "sealing" the finished mole paste.
*   **Liquid:** Chicken or turkey broth is used to thin the paste into a sauce and allow it to simmer.

The quality and freshness of each ingredient are paramount for an authentic and delicious Oaxacan Black Mole.

## How to Make Oaxacan Black Mole: Step-by-Step

Preparing Oaxacan Black Mole is a labor of love, but the rewarding flavor makes every step worthwhile. Here's a simplified overview of the traditional process:

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Destem and deseed all dried chiles. Lightly toast them in a dry comal or skillet until fragrant, being careful not to burn them. Rehydrate them by soaking in hot water until softened, then drain.
2.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds & Spices:** Individually toast almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, and all whole spices (cinnamon, cloves, peppercorns, cumin, etc.) in a dry pan until aromatic. Set aside.
3.  **Fry Other Solids:** In a bit of lard or oil, fry sliced plantains, raisins, prunes, torn tortillas, and bread until golden. Separately, sauté chopped onion and garlic until softened.
4.  **Blend in Batches:** Combine the rehydrated chiles with a bit of their soaking liquid and blend until very smooth. Strain the mixture to remove any tough skins or seeds, ensuring a silky texture. Then, blend the toasted nuts, seeds, spices, fried fruits, bread, and sautéed aromatics with some broth until a thick, smooth paste forms.
5.  **Cook the Paste ("Sazonar"):** This is a crucial step. In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or cazuela with a generous amount of lard or oil, add the blended chile paste. Cook it over medium heat, stirring constantly, for at least 30-45 minutes, or even longer, until it darkens significantly and the oil separates. This process, known as "sazonar" or seasoning, deeply develops the flavors.
6.  **Combine & Simmer:** Add the blended nut/seed/spice paste to the chile paste. Stir well to combine. Gradually stir in the chicken or turkey broth, adjusting for desired consistency. Add the Mexican chocolate, breaking it into pieces to help it melt.
7.  **Slow Simmer:** Bring the mole to a gentle simmer. Reduce heat to low and cook, stirring occasionally, for at least 1-2 hours, or even longer. This allows the flavors to meld and deepen. Taste and adjust seasoning, adding salt, a touch more sugar, or vinegar if needed. The Oaxacan Black Mole should be thick, rich, and glossy.

## Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

Crafting an exceptional Oaxacan Black Mole is an art form. Here are some tips to elevate your creation:

*   **Source Quality Ingredients:** This cannot be stressed enough. Fresh, aromatic chiles, good quality Mexican chocolate, and fresh spices make a world of difference. Seek out chilhuacle negro chiles if you can find them; their unique smoky flavor is key.
*   **Don't Rush the Toasting:** Each ingredient needs careful attention during toasting. Too little, and flavors won't develop; too much, and they'll burn and become bitter. Toast individually, as different ingredients have different toasting times.
*   **Strain, Strain, Strain:** After blending the chiles, strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve. This removes any fibrous bits, ensuring the silky smooth texture that defines a great mole. You might need to add a little broth to help it pass through.
*   **The "Sazonar" Step is Critical:** Frying the chile paste in lard or oil for an extended period is vital for deepening the color and flavor. It transforms the raw chile flavor into something rich and complex. Don't skip or shorten this step!
*   **Balance is Key:** Oaxacan Black Mole is a delicate dance between sweet, savory, spicy, and earthy. Taste frequently throughout the simmering process and adjust with salt, sugar, or a touch of vinegar to achieve harmony.
*   **Patience is a Virtue:** This dish truly benefits from slow cooking. The longer it simmers (without burning), the more the flavors will meld and intensify.
*   **Make it Ahead:** Like many stews and complex sauces, Oaxacan Black Mole often tastes even better the next day, allowing all the flavors to fully integrate.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Once your Oaxacan Black Mole is perfected, the traditional way to serve it is simple, allowing the sauce to be the star of the show.

*   **With Poultry:** The most classic pairing is with poached or roasted chicken or turkey pieces. The rich sauce beautifully complements the tender meat.
*   **White Rice:** A simple side of fluffy white rice is essential. It provides a neutral canvas to soak up every drop of the exquisite mole.
*   **Warm Tortillas:** Freshly made corn tortillas are a must. They're perfect for scooping up the mole and meat, creating delicious, informal tacos.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, a few sprigs of fresh cilantro, or thinly sliced red onion can add texture and a touch of freshness.
*   **Beyond Chicken:** While poultry is traditional, don't limit yourself! Mole Negro can be served over pork loin, roasted vegetables (like sweet potatoes or cauliflower for a vegetarian option), or even firm tofu.
*   **Enmoladas:** A popular way to enjoy leftover mole is to warm tortillas, dip them in the mole, fill them with shredded chicken or cheese, roll them up, and then top with more mole and garnishes – similar to enchiladas, but with mole!
*   **Tamales:** Mole Negro also makes an incredible filling for tamales.

## Variations & Substitutions for Black Mole

While the traditional recipe for Oaxacan Black Mole is sacred, some adaptations can be made based on ingredient availability or dietary preferences.

*   **Chiles:** If chilhuacle negro chiles are impossible to find, a combination of mulato, ancho, and pasilla chiles with a touch of chipotle (for smokiness) can be used, though the flavor profile will differ slightly.
*   **Nuts and Seeds:** While almonds, peanuts, and sesame seeds are standard, you can experiment with other nuts like cashews or even sunflower seeds for slight variations in texture and flavor.
*   **Sweeteners:** Piloncillo is traditional, but dark brown sugar can be used. Adjust the amount based on your preference and the sweetness of your chocolate.
*   **Chocolate:** High-quality Mexican chocolate is ideal. If unavailable, use a good quality dark chocolate (70-80% cacao) and add a pinch of ground cinnamon and a bit more sugar.
*   **Fat:** While lard offers an unparalleled richness, a neutral oil like avocado or canola oil can be used for a lighter version.
*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** For a plant-based Mole Negro, substitute chicken broth with vegetable broth. Instead of meat, serve it over roasted root vegetables, mushrooms, or black beans. Ensure your Mexican chocolate is dairy-free, as some brands contain milk.
*   **Time-Saving:** For those with limited time, some specialty stores sell pre-made Oaxacan Black Mole paste. While convenient, it won't quite match the complexity and freshness of a homemade batch, but it can be a good starting point.

Creating Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary adventure, a true labor of love that results in one of the most magnificent Mexican dinner recipes imaginable. Its rich history, complex flavors, and celebratory spirit make it a dish truly worth savoring.
